name: Peña Amaya Loop from Alar del Rey
name_original: Peña Amaya – circular desde Alar del Rey por Salazar de Amaya y Rebolledo de la Torre
description: This difficult 35.1-mile touring cycling route explores the Las Loras Geopark, offering historical sites and panoramic views from Peña Amaya
region_intro: Touring cycling around Alar Del Rey features routes that traverse varied terrain, including the historic Canal de Castilla and areas with Romanesque architecture. The landscape is characterized by gentle river valleys, agricultural plains, and some elevated sections, such as those near Peña Amaya. This region offers a mix of flat, scenic paths and more undulating routes, providing diverse experiences for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Alar Del Rey The most popular touring cycling route is Peña Amaya Loop from Alar del Rey, a 35.1 miles (56.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 37 minutes to complete, offering significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Palencian Romanesque Trail, a difficult 13.7 miles (22.1 km) path. This route explores areas rich in historical architecture and cultural landmarks. Local touring cyclists also love the Boedo-Ojeda and Canal de Castilla Loop, a 32.9 miles (53.0 km) trail leading through the scenic Canal de Castilla, often completed in about 3 hours 22 minutes. Touring cycling around Alar Del Rey is defined by historic canals, Romanesque sites, and rolling hills, with routes available for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Alar Del Rey are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 15 reviews. More than 190 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Alar Del Rey's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Alar Del Rey?
A: There are over 35 touring cycling routes available around Alar Del Rey, catering to various skill levels from easy to difficult. This includes routes along the historic Canal de Castilla and through areas rich in Romanesque architecture.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Alar Del Rey offers several easy touring cycling routes. While specific family-friendly routes aren't detailed, the region's gentle river valleys and agricultural plains provide suitable terrain. For a moderate option that is less challenging than some of the longer routes, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170921639/palencian-romanesque-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palencian Romanesque Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 30.1 km long and explores historical sites.

Q: What are some of the longer touring cycling routes in the Alar Del Rey area?
A: For longer rides, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1174374170/pena-amaya-loop-from-alar-del-rey" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Amaya Loop from Alar del Rey&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 56.5 km route with significant elevation gain. Another substantial option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10506610" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of San Miguel – Pisuerga River loop from Aguilar de Campoo&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 57.5 km and also features considerable climbing.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes around Alar Del Rey?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops. A notable circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175214598/boedo-ojeda-and-canal-de-castilla-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boedo-Ojeda and Canal de Castilla Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 53.0 km long and takes you through the scenic Canal de Castilla. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170921639/palencian-romanesque-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palencian Romanesque Loop&lt;/a&gt; is another excellent circular option.

Q: What kind of historical or natural attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in both history and natural beauty. You can explore areas with Romanesque architecture, especially along routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170933618/palencian-romanesque-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palencian Romanesque Trail&lt;/a&gt;. Nearby natural attractions include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4413098" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Mesa&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4045496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Horadada Tunnel&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4381459" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;CastroAmaya&lt;/a&gt; historical site. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4586753" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Mesa Rock Formation (Las Tuerces)&lt;/a&gt; is also a significant natural monument.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Alar Del Rey?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Alar Del Rey is generally during spring and autumn when the weather is mild and the landscapes are vibrant. Summers can be warm, especially in the open agricultural plains, while winters can be cold and potentially wet, making some routes less comfortable.

Q: What are the typical terrain conditions for touring cycling in Alar Del Rey?
A: The terrain around Alar Del Rey is varied. You'll find flat, scenic paths along the historic Canal de Castilla, agricultural plains, and some more undulating or elevated sections, particularly near areas like Peña Amaya. Routes often combine paved roads with well-maintained gravel paths, suitable for touring bicycles.

Q: Are there good viewpoints along the touring cycling routes in Alar Del Rey?
A: Yes, the region offers several scenic viewpoints, especially in elevated areas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5812303" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Peña Amaya and Castillo de Peña Amaya&lt;/a&gt; is a notable highlight, offering panoramic vistas of the landscape and historical structures. Routes that traverse higher ground, such as the Peña Amaya Loop, will provide numerous scenic opportunities.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about their experience in Alar Del Rey?
A: The touring cycling routes around Alar Del Rey are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 15 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, the historical significance of the Romanesque sites, and the unique experience of riding along the Canal de Castilla.

Q: Is Alar Del Rey suitable for advanced touring cyclists looking for a challenge?
A: Absolutely. Alar Del Rey offers several difficult routes for experienced touring cyclists. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1174374170/pena-amaya-loop-from-alar-del-rey" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Amaya Loop from Alar del Rey&lt;/a&gt;, at 56.5 km with significant elevation gain, is a prime example of a challenging ride. There are 19 routes classified as difficult, providing ample opportunities for advanced riders.

Q: Are there any specific considerations for cycling along the Canal de Castilla?
A: Cycling along the Canal de Castilla offers a unique experience on mostly flat, well-maintained towpaths. While generally easy, some sections might be unpaved, so a touring bike with suitable tires is recommended. The canal paths are excellent for enjoying the tranquil scenery and historical engineering. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175214598/boedo-ojeda-and-canal-de-castilla-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boedo-Ojeda and Canal de Castilla Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a great way to experience this.
tips: The Canal de Castilla in Alar del Rey is an impressive testimony of 18th century hydraulic engineering, where its locks and aqueducts cross the town.

From this point you can enjoy excellent views of the majestic Peña de Amaya. With an altitude of 1,377 meters, it has been inhabited since Prehistory and reached its maximum splendor at the end of the 10th century BC. From then on, Amaya became one of the main Cantabrian castros during the Iron Age.

Late Romanesque style temple from the 12th century. Although it has suffered damage, part of the original chancel with three apses is preserved, one of which has been replaced by a tower. Inside, the ogival vault from the 13th century stands out. The reconstruction of 1905 gave it a neo-Lombard appearance decorated with blind brick arches.

At this place the Canal de Castilla begins its journey - a masterpiece of Spanish hydraulic engineering from the 18th / 19th century. The canal is 207 km long. Here we can see the commemorative monolith for the 200 years of the North Branch of the canal, installed in 1991.

The power of water, the small town of Alar del Rey is literally surrounded by water. The beginning of the Canal de Castilla, an industrial attempt to boost trade by taking advantage of the power of the river during the 18th century. Today, it is a cultural and sports center with the canal a site of cultural importance.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route features a mixed terrain, combining paved roads connecting villages with unpaved sections known as "caminos." Cyclists should expect gravel, dirt, and other unpaved surfaces, especially when venturing off main roads. A gravel bike or mountain bike equipped for touring is recommended over a traditional road touring bike due to these varied surfaces.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers several notable sights. You can see the Canal de Castilla in Alar del Rey, enjoy a View of Peña Amaya, visit the Church of San Julián and Santa Basilisa, and explore the Source of the Canal de Castilla. Don't miss the Dock and Locks of the Canal de Castilla in Alar del Rey.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Alar del Rey. Parking should be available within the village, which serves as a convenient starting point for the circular tour.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Considering the mixed terrain and the region's climate, spring and autumn are generally the best times to cycle. These seasons offer more moderate temperatures and pleasant conditions for exploring the varied landscapes and historical sites.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through natural areas and villages, specific regulations for dogs on cycling trails can vary. It's advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in populated areas or near livestock, and to be mindful of local rules regarding pets in public spaces.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are required to cycle this route itself. However, the trail is located within the Las Loras UNESCO Global Geopark, so it's important to respect environmental regulations and local guidelines.

faqs.question: What should I know about cycling within the Las Loras UNESCO Global Geopark?
faqs.answer: The route traverses the Las Loras UNESCO Global Geopark, a region recognized for its unique geological formations and historical significance. When cycling here, please respect the natural environment, stay on marked paths, and be aware of local wildlife. There are no specific entrance fees for the Geopark itself, but responsible tourism practices are encouraged.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or resupply along the way?
faqs.answer: The route connects several charming villages, including Alar del Rey, Salazar de Amaya, and Rebolledo de la Torre. These villages offer opportunities to find local amenities, cafes, or small shops where you can resupply with food and water.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. You'll encounter sections of R2 - Peones de Amaya, R3 - Valle de Riomance, the Canal de Castilla (Ramal Norte-Sur), GR-89 BTT Canal de Castilla (Ramal Norte Etapa Alar - Frómista), and R1 - Valdeamaya, among others.

faqs.question: What kind of bike is best suited for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the mixed terrain, which includes both paved roads and unpaved "caminos" (gravel/dirt tracks), a gravel bike or a mountain bike equipped for touring would be ideal. A traditional road touring bike might struggle with the rougher sections.

faqs.question: What are the historical highlights of the area?
faqs.answer: The area is rich in history, with Peña Amaya itself being a strategic stronghold for various civilizations including the Cantabrians, Romans, Visigoths, and Arabs. Cycling through this region offers a journey through time, with archaeological remains and historical villages providing a deep sense of the past.
